title: IELTS Academic Reading Jan 2026 Passage 3 - Urban Evolution: When Cities Become the New Galapagos
rawPassageText:
READING PASSAGE 3
You should spend about 20 minutes on Questions 27-40, which are based on Reading Passage 3 below.

Urban Evolution: When Cities Become the New Galapagos

A
For over a century, the narrative surrounding urbanization has been one of ecological devastation. Biologists and conservationists have traditionally viewed cities as biological dead zones-vast expanses of asphalt and concrete that eradicate natural habitats and push wildlife toward extinction. However, a revolutionary shift is occurring in evolutionary biology. Researchers are increasingly recognizing that cities are not just destroying nature; they are actively shaping it. Rather than being biological wastelands, modern metropolises act as intense evolutionary pressure cookers, forcing wildlife to adapt and mutate at an unprecedented and staggering pace.

B
Dr. Aris Menelaou, an evolutionary ecologist, has been studying this phenomenon in the urban crested anoles-a species of lizard native to Puerto Rico. Menelaou compared the anatomy of lizards living in the dense, concrete infrastructure of San Juan with those living in the island's remote rainforests. He discovered a remarkable physical divergence that had occurred in just a few decades. The urban lizards had developed significantly longer limbs and developed specialized, ultra-sticky scales on their toe pads. "In the rainforest, short legs are ideal for navigating thin, uneven branches," Menelaou explains. "But in the city, long legs and sticky pads are required to sprint vertically up smooth, painted concrete walls and glass windows to escape predators."

C
The mechanisms driving this rapid evolution are the extreme microclimates generated by human activity. Cities are subject to the Urban Heat Island effect*, meaning they are significantly warmer than surrounding rural areas due to heat-absorbing building materials. Furthermore, they are saturated with artificial light, chemical pollutants, and constant noise. To survive, animals must undergo rapid physiological adjustments. Moths in industrial cities have famously shifted their wing coloration to match soot-covered buildings, a process known as industrial melanism. Today, researchers are documenting fish in urban rivers that have evolved distinct genetic immunities to lethal industrial toxins that would instantly kill their rural counterparts.

D
However, this evolutionary adaptability is not entirely a success story. Dr. Kenji Tanaka, a population geneticist, warns that urban adaptation often comes at a severe, hidden cost. When a species undergoes rapid evolution to survive in a city, it frequently experiences a "genetic bottleneck." Because only a small percentage of the original population possesses the specific traits needed to survive the harsh urban transition, the overall genetic diversity of the species plummets. "These urban creatures become highly specialized to a very specific, man-made environment," Tanaka warns. "While they thrive today, this dramatic loss of genetic variation leaves them incredibly vulnerable. If the urban environment changes abruptly, they no longer have the genetic tools to adapt again."

E
The scope of urban evolution extends beyond anatomy, reaching deeply into animal behavior and neurobiology. Dr. Fiona Clark, an ornithologist, has conducted extensive research on European blackbirds living in noisy metropolitan centers. Clark found that city birds have fundamentally altered the pitch of their songs, singing at noticeably higher frequencies than forest birds to ensure their mating calls cut through the low-frequency rumble of traffic. More surprisingly, Clark's team took blood samples and discovered a permanent genetic mutation in the urban birds. This mutation suppresses their baseline cortisol production. This internal biological shift allows the birds to withstand the constant visual and auditory disturbances of the city without dying from chronic stress.

F
These findings are sparking a fierce debate regarding the future of environmental conservation. Historically, conservation efforts have focused almost exclusively on preserving pristine, untouched wilderness. But as human populations explode and cities expand, scientists like Clark argue for a shift toward "reconciliation ecology"-the science of redesigning human habitats to accommodate wildlife. If species are already adapting to our cities, ecologists argue that urban planners should intentionally design infrastructure that supports these new, highly evolved urban ecosystems, rather than trying to keep the "wild" strictly separated from the "civilized."

G
Ultimately, the study of urban evolution forces humanity to rethink its place in the natural world. We can no longer view ourselves merely as the destroyers of habitats; we must recognize our role as the primary architects of a new evolutionary trajectory. The concrete jungles we have built are acting as the new Galapagos Islands, driving a wave of rapid, human-directed natural selection. The wildlife of tomorrow will not be a relic of the ancient past, but a reflection of the cities we are building today.

*Urban Heat Island effect: a metropolitan area that is significantly warmer than its surrounding rural areas due to human activities and infrastructure.

Questions 27-31
Reading Passage 3 has seven paragraphs, A-G.
Which paragraph contains the following information?
Write the correct letter, A-G, in boxes 27-31 on your answer sheet.

27. an explanation of how concentrated human activity and building materials alter local temperatures
28. a warning about the long-term vulnerability of highly specialized, city-dwelling creatures
29. the suggestion that traditional environmental protection has focused too heavily on untouched wilderness
30. a contrast between a behavioral modification and a permanent change to internal body chemistry
31. an example of an anatomical physical change driven by modern building surfaces

Questions 32-35
Look at the following statements (Questions 32-35) and the list of researchers below.
Match each statement with the correct researcher, A, B, or C.
Write the correct letter, A, B, or C, in boxes 32-35 on your answer sheet.
NB You may use any letter more than once.

List of Researchers
A  Aris Menelaou
B  Kenji Tanaka
C  Fiona Clark

32. A severe reduction in DNA diversity leaves species susceptible to sudden environmental shifts.
33. Animals can adjust their physical appendages to better navigate man-made architecture.
34. City-dwelling creatures modify their vocalizations to overcome human-generated noise pollution.
35. The rapid transition into urban environments causes a dramatic drop in the overall genetic variety of a species.

Questions 36-39
Complete the summary below.
Choose ONE WORD ONLY from the passage for each answer.
Write your answers in boxes 36-39 on your answer sheet.

Adaptations in Urban Blackbirds

Dr. Fiona Clark's research demonstrates that city wildlife adapts both behaviorally and genetically. She observed that urban blackbirds alter the 36 ____________ of their songs to be heard over the continuous noise of traffic. However, these adaptations go beyond vocal changes. Clark discovered that these birds have developed a genetic 37 ____________ that suppresses their cortisol levels. This crucial biological adjustment helps the birds endure the daily 38 ____________ found in metropolitan areas without succumbing to chronic stress. Ultimately, Clark's findings prove that cities are sparking a fierce 39 ____________ among scientists about how we should manage and design future conservation efforts.

Question 40
Choose the correct letter, A, B, C or D.
Write the correct letter in box 40 on your answer sheet.

40. What is the writer's main purpose in Reading Passage 3?
A to argue that urban expansion is completely toxic to all forms of wildlife
B to explain how man-made environments are driving rapid evolutionary changes in animal species
C to criticize urban planners for failing to incorporate sufficient green spaces into modern cities
D to demonstrate that behavioral adaptations are significantly more important than genetic mutations
rawAnswerKey:
Question 27: an explanation of how concentrated human activity and building materials alter local temperatures

Correct Answer: C

Accepted Answers: C

Answer Group: IELTS Academic Reading Jan 2026 Passage 3 - Urban Evolution: When Cities Become the New Galapagos

Exact Portion: Cities are subject to the Urban Heat Island effect*, meaning they are significantly warmer than surrounding rural areas due to heat-absorbing building materials.

Short Thai Explanation: คำตอบคือ C เพราะย่อหน้า C อธิบาย Urban Heat Island และ heat-absorbing building materials ที่ทำให้เมืองร้อนกว่าชนบท โจทย์ถาม concentrated human activity + building materials → alter temperatures ตรงกับ microclimates จาก human activity ในย่อหน้า C ไม่ใช่ย่อหน้า A ที่เป็น overview

Paraphrased Vocabulary: building materials = heat-absorbing building materials; alter local temperatures = significantly warmer than surrounding rural areas

Question 28: a warning about the long-term vulnerability of highly specialized, city-dwelling creatures

Correct Answer: D

Accepted Answers: D

Answer Group: IELTS Academic Reading Jan 2026 Passage 3 - Urban Evolution: When Cities Become the New Galapagos

Exact Portion: this dramatic loss of genetic variation leaves them incredibly vulnerable. If the urban environment changes abruptly, they no longer have the genetic tools to adapt again.

Short Thai Explanation: คำตอบคือ D เพราะย่อหน้า D Tanaka เตือนว่า genetic variation ลดลงทำให้ urban creatures vulnerable เมื่อ environment changes abruptly โจทย์ถาม long-term vulnerability ของ highly specialized city-dwelling creatures ตรงกับย่อหน้า D ไม่ใช่ย่อหน้า C ที่เน้นแค่ adaptation สำเร็จ

Paraphrased Vocabulary: long-term vulnerability = incredibly vulnerable / no longer have the genetic tools; highly specialized = highly specialized to a man-made environment

Question 29: the suggestion that traditional environmental protection has focused too heavily on untouched wilderness

Correct Answer: F

Accepted Answers: F

Answer Group: IELTS Academic Reading Jan 2026 Passage 3 - Urban Evolution: When Cities Become the New Galapagos

Exact Portion: Historically, conservation efforts have focused almost exclusively on preserving pristine, untouched wilderness.

Short Thai Explanation: คำตอบคือ F เพราะย่อหน้า F ระบุว่า conservation efforts historically focused almost exclusively on preserving pristine, untouched wilderness ก่อนเสนอ reconciliation ecology โจทย์ถาม traditional protection ที่เน้น untouched wilderness มากเกินไป ตรงย่อหน้า F

Paraphrased Vocabulary: traditional environmental protection = conservation efforts historically; untouched wilderness = pristine, untouched wilderness

Question 30: a contrast between a behavioral modification and a permanent change to internal body chemistry

Correct Answer: E

Accepted Answers: E

Answer Group: IELTS Academic Reading Jan 2026 Passage 3 - Urban Evolution: When Cities Become the New Galapagos

Exact Portion: Clark found that city birds have fundamentally altered the pitch of their songs, singing at noticeably higher frequencies than forest birds to ensure their mating calls cut through the low-frequency rumble of traffic. More surprisingly, Clark's team took blood samples and discovered a permanent genetic mutation in the urban birds. This mutation suppresses their baseline cortisol production.

Short Thai Explanation: คำตอบคือ E เพราะย่อหน้า E เปรียบ behavioral change (altered pitch) กับ permanent genetic mutation (cortisol) ของ Clark โจทย์ถาม contrast ระหว่าง behavioral modification กับ internal body chemistry ตรงย่อหน้า E ไม่ใช่ย่อหน้า B ที่เป็นแค่ anatomy ของกิ้งก่า

Paraphrased Vocabulary: behavioral modification = altered the pitch of their songs; permanent change to body chemistry = permanent genetic mutation / cortisol production

Question 31: an example of an anatomical physical change driven by modern building surfaces

Correct Answer: B

Accepted Answers: B

Answer Group: IELTS Academic Reading Jan 2026 Passage 3 - Urban Evolution: When Cities Become the New Galapagos

Exact Portion: The urban lizards had developed significantly longer limbs and developed specialized, ultra-sticky scales on their toe pads. "But in the city, long legs and sticky pads are required to sprint vertically up smooth, painted concrete walls and glass windows to escape predators."

Short Thai Explanation: คำตอบคือ B เพราะย่อหน้า B ยกตัวอย่าง longer limbs และ sticky toe pads เพื่อปีน concrete walls และ glass windows โจทย์ถาม anatomical change จาก modern building surfaces ตรง Menelaou ในย่อหน้า B

Paraphrased Vocabulary: anatomical physical change = longer limbs / ultra-sticky scales; modern building surfaces = concrete walls and glass windows

Question 32: A severe reduction in DNA diversity leaves species susceptible to sudden environmental shifts.

Correct Answer: B

Accepted Answers: B

Answer Group: IELTS Academic Reading Jan 2026 Passage 3 - Urban Evolution: When Cities Become the New Galapagos

Exact Portion: the overall genetic diversity of the species plummets. "While they thrive today, this dramatic loss of genetic variation leaves them incredibly vulnerable. If the urban environment changes abruptly, they no longer have the genetic tools to adapt again."

Short Thai Explanation: คำตอบคือ B เพราะโจทย์พูด DNA diversity ลด → susceptible to sudden shifts ซึ่งตรง Kenji Tanaka เรื่อง genetic diversity plummets และ vulnerable if environment changes abruptly ไม่ใช่ Menelaou (anatomy) หรือ Clark (birdsong)

Paraphrased Vocabulary: DNA diversity = genetic diversity / genetic variation; sudden environmental shifts = urban environment changes abruptly; researcher = Kenji Tanaka

Question 33: Animals can adjust their physical appendages to better navigate man-made architecture.

Correct Answer: A

Accepted Answers: A

Answer Group: IELTS Academic Reading Jan 2026 Passage 3 - Urban Evolution: When Cities Become the New Galapagos

Exact Portion: The urban lizards had developed significantly longer limbs and developed specialized, ultra-sticky scales on their toe pads.

Short Thai Explanation: คำตอบคือ A เพราะโจทย์พูด physical appendages → man-made architecture ซึ่งตรง Aris Menelaou เรื่อง limbs และ toe pads สำหรับ concrete walls ไม่ใช่ Tanaka หรือ Clark

Paraphrased Vocabulary: physical appendages = limbs / toe pads; man-made architecture = concrete walls and glass windows; researcher = Aris Menelaou

Question 34: City-dwelling creatures modify their vocalizations to overcome human-generated noise pollution.

Correct Answer: C

Accepted Answers: C

Answer Group: IELTS Academic Reading Jan 2026 Passage 3 - Urban Evolution: When Cities Become the New Galapagos

Exact Portion: Clark found that city birds have fundamentally altered the pitch of their songs, singing at noticeably higher frequencies than forest birds to ensure their mating calls cut through the low-frequency rumble of traffic.

Short Thai Explanation: คำตอบคือ C เพราะโจทย์พูด modify vocalizations → overcome noise pollution ซึ่งตรง Fiona Clark เรื่อง higher frequency songs และ traffic rumble ไม่ใช่ Menelaou หรือ Tanaka

Paraphrased Vocabulary: modify vocalizations = altered the pitch / higher frequencies; noise pollution = low-frequency rumble of traffic; researcher = Fiona Clark

Question 35: The rapid transition into urban environments causes a dramatic drop in the overall genetic variety of a species.

Correct Answer: B

Accepted Answers: B

Answer Group: IELTS Academic Reading Jan 2026 Passage 3 - Urban Evolution: When Cities Become the New Galapagos

Exact Portion: When a species undergoes rapid evolution to survive in a city, it frequently experiences a "genetic bottleneck." Because only a small percentage of the original population possesses the specific traits needed to survive the harsh urban transition, the overall genetic diversity of the species plummets.

Short Thai Explanation: คำตอบคือ B เพราะโจทย์พูด rapid urban transition → drop in genetic variety ซึ่งตรง genetic bottleneck ของ Kenji Tanaka (ใช้ B ซ้ำได้ตาม NB) ไม่ใช่ Menelaou ที่พูดแค่ physical divergence

Paraphrased Vocabulary: rapid transition = harsh urban transition; genetic variety = genetic diversity; researcher = Kenji Tanaka / genetic bottleneck

Question 36: urban blackbirds alter the .................... of their songs to be heard over the continuous noise of traffic.

Correct Answer: pitch

Accepted Answers: pitch

Answer Group: IELTS Academic Reading Jan 2026 Passage 3 - Urban Evolution: When Cities Become the New Galapagos

Exact Portion: city birds have fundamentally altered the pitch of their songs

Short Thai Explanation: คำตอบคือ pitch เพราะ summary ถาม alter the ___ of their songs ซึ่ง passage ใช้ altered the pitch of their songs ต้องตอบ pitch เท่านั้น (ONE WORD ONLY) ไม่ใช้ frequency หรือ songs

Paraphrased Vocabulary: alter the ___ of their songs = altered the pitch; ONE WORD = pitch

Question 37: these birds have developed a genetic .................... that suppresses their cortisol levels.

Correct Answer: mutation

Accepted Answers: mutation

Answer Group: IELTS Academic Reading Jan 2026 Passage 3 - Urban Evolution: When Cities Become the New Galapagos

Exact Portion: discovered a permanent genetic mutation in the urban birds. This mutation suppresses their baseline cortisol production.

Short Thai Explanation: คำตอบคือ mutation เพราะ summary ถาม genetic ___ ที่ suppresses cortisol ซึ่ง passage ระบุ permanent genetic mutation ต้องตอบ mutation คำเดียว ไม่ใช่ cortisol เพราะ blank ต้องการชนิดของการเปลี่ยนแปลง ไม่ใช่ฮอร์โมน

Paraphrased Vocabulary: genetic ___ = permanent genetic mutation; suppresses cortisol = suppresses baseline cortisol production; ONE WORD = mutation

Question 38: helps the birds endure the daily .................... found in metropolitan areas without succumbing to chronic stress.

Correct Answer: disturbances

Accepted Answers: disturbances

Answer Group: IELTS Academic Reading Jan 2026 Passage 3 - Urban Evolution: When Cities Become the New Galapagos

Exact Portion: withstand the constant visual and auditory disturbances of the city without dying from chronic stress

Short Thai Explanation: คำตอบคือ disturbances เพราะ summary ถาม daily ___ ใน metropolitan areas ซึ่งตรง constant visual and auditory disturbances ใน passage คำว่า stress หรือ noise ไม่ตรงวลีในบทความ

Paraphrased Vocabulary: daily ___ = constant visual and auditory disturbances; metropolitan areas = the city; ONE WORD = disturbances

Question 39: cities are sparking a fierce .................... among scientists about how we should manage and design future conservation efforts.

Correct Answer: debate

Accepted Answers: debate

Answer Group: IELTS Academic Reading Jan 2026 Passage 3 - Urban Evolution: When Cities Become the New Galapagos

Exact Portion: These findings are sparking a fierce debate regarding the future of environmental conservation.

Short Thai Explanation: คำตอบคือ debate เพราะ summary ถาม fierce ___ among scientists ซึ่งตรง sparking a fierce debate ในย่อหน้า F (แม้ Clark ถูกกล่าวถึงในย่อหน้าเดียวกัน แต่คำในช่องว่างคือ debate จากประโยค These findings are sparking...) ไม่ใช่ ecology หรือ conservation เพราะต้อง ONE WORD ONLY

Paraphrased Vocabulary: fierce ___ = fierce debate; among scientists = regarding the future of environmental conservation; ONE WORD = debate

Question 40: What is the writer's main purpose in Reading Passage 3?

Correct Answer: B

Accepted Answers: B

Answer Group: IELTS Academic Reading Jan 2026 Passage 3 - Urban Evolution: When Cities Become the New Galapagos

Exact Portion: modern metropolises act as intense evolutionary pressure cookers, forcing wildlife to adapt and mutate at an unprecedented and staggering pace.

Short Thai Explanation: คำตอบคือ B เพราะทั้งบทความอธิบายว่าเมืองเป็น evolutionary pressure cookers ที่ขับ adapt/mutate ตัวเลือก A ขัดกับ cities shape nature ตัวเลือก C วิจารณ์ planners ที่ไม่ใช่โฟกัสหลัก ตัวเลือก D เปรียบ behavioral vs genetic ที่ passage ไม่ได้สรุปว่าอย่างใดสำคัญกว่า

Paraphrased Vocabulary: main purpose = man-made environments driving rapid evolution; B = pressure cookers / adapt and mutate; A/C/D = not main writer purpose
